meta.title: Video by jinabaobina
meta.description:
OG Ktown Part 2: Korean comfort food

📍 Olympic Noodle in Koreatown LA

What I ordered (Total $54 for 3 dishes)

Sujebi: hand torn dough soup. Good but not my favorite. The ratios were off to me - Too much potato and chicken, not enough hand torn dough, which is my favorite part. Also the soup contents were so big and chunky taking away from the ease of enjoying a typically minimal dish. I will say, the dough itself had a really nice chewy texture which I enjoyed.

Steamed dumplings: Heavy on chives in the best way. The flavor really comes out when you dip it in the sauce as the soy complements the filling nicely 

Fried dumplings: I thought these would be crispy, but they were more chewy. Sometimes this happens when being fried from frozen due to the water on the surface that ends up steaming the dough instead of crisping it.

Kimchi: So good. Super flavorful, and still had that crunch. I’d come back just for this. Sometimes the really flavorful kimchis feel soggy from soaking for a really long time but somehow this one retained its texture AND all of the flavor

I do wish I tried more, but going solo, there was no way I could finish everything

For this series, I don’t want to focus too much on the negatives. I still want to be honest, but in my voiceovers I want to highlight the positives as much as I can.

At the end of the day, everyone has different tastes. If this video helps you find something you end up loving here, that’s what matters most

And just showing up to these places and supporting the history of Koreatown means a lot too

#koreanfood #losangeles #lafoodie #lafood #koreannoodles

transcript.transcript: Welcome to the OG restaurants that built Korea Town, a series honoring the spots that made Korea Town what it is today. This is where you can find Ajima serving true Korean comfort food. The kind that feels like home. It's been open since the 1990s, this is Olympic noodle. They're known for Kaguksu or Knife Cut Noodles, but when I saw that they had Sujabi, hand-torned dosu, I had to order it. It's a dish I cherished growing up, and when I always asked my mom to make when I visit home. I was told to get the mandu, so I decided to try both the fried and steamed to compare. Between the two, I would get the steamed. I'll share more details about the food in the caption. The kimchi is so good. I would honestly come back just for that. Perfectly crunchy while holding the flavor of the marinade. Places like this feel like eating a piece of Kree Town's history. Being surrounded by Koreans, served by Ajima's, it honestly felt like I was back in Korea. This isn't one of the best meals I've ever had, but I'm really glad I came. Because sometimes it's not just about the food, it's about the feeling, and this place gives you that sense of comfort. Would you try Olympic noodle?
